exemplar.title: AI Customer Support Chatbot for Dentistry

detailedExemplarData.title: AI Customer Support Chatbot for Dentistry

strengths: Clear identification of client and scenario with evidence of two consultations
Relevant rationale linking technology choices to project aims
Well-defined, specific success criteria mapping directly to user needs
weaknesses: Consultation evidence lacks dates, detailed notes or appendices
Rationale does not compare alternatives in depth
Success criteria lack measurable thresholds (e.g., response times or accuracy targets)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the planning is solidly in the 3â4 band. The student identifies Health 1 as the client and quotes both administrator and CEO, but omits supporting documentation. The rationale for chosen tools shows awareness of security and efficiency but is not fully justified by comparing alternatives. Success criteria are appropriate and testable yet lack concrete performance thresholds. A score of 4 reflects this mid-band quality.

strengths: High level of complexity and ingenuity in orchestrating WhatsAppâManyChatâFlaskâOpenAI GPT and YClients APIs
Modular code structure, use of decorators and non-trivial algorithmic decisions
Appropriate and effective use of multiple tools and libraries with clear explanations of key techniques
weaknesses: Minor typos and inconsistent variable names that could lead to runtime errors
Inline citations and docstrings are sparseâdocumentation could be more comprehensive
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs development demonstrates high complexity and effective tool use, integrating multiple third-party services and handling multilingual input and error cases. While there are minor polish issues, the overall work clearly fits the 9â12 band. Given the sophistication of design and implementation, a full 12/12 is awarded.

strengths: Comprehensive set of five flowcharts showing user interaction, server/client, hardware, back-end logic and appointment process
Highly detailed test plan mapping every success criterion to test data, expected outcome and pass/fail conditions
weaknesses: No record of tasks (e.g., Gantt chart or development log) to show project timeline
Minor layout issues in diagrams and missing legend for flowchart symbols
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design overview and test plan are both very strong (bands 5â6), but the absence of any record of tasks (band 0) prevents a perfect score. Holistically the work sits at the high end of the 3â4 band, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Core functionality for FAQ, appointment booking and rescheduling is implemented and demonstrated via screenshots
Modular code suggests potential for future extension
weaknesses: Tight coupling to hard-coded IDs makes expansion non-trivial
Lack of video or execution log limits direct evidence of full functionality
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Functionality works in key areas but evidence is indirect, placing it at the top of the 1â2 band. Extensibility is possible but requires effort. A score of 3/4 reflects solid partial functionality with reasonable prospects for straightforward modification if improved.

weaknesses: No evaluation of outcomes against success criteria
No client or adviser feedback provided
No future recommendations or reflective planning included
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission contains no evaluation section. There is no systematic comparison with success criteria, no documented stakeholder feedback and no suggestions for future improvements, justifying 0/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Five detailed flow-charts (user interaction, server-client, hardware, back-end, appointment process) plus an interface mock-up give a holistic picture of structure, data flow and user journey.  Despite minor layout issues, the diagrams are logically linked and sufficiently annotated to guide implementation, satisfying the âcomprehensive overviewâ descriptor.

The rationale explains why Python, ManyChat, YClients and ChatGPT were chosen and links them to the aims of reducing human error and improving response speed.  It acknowledges security and privacy concerns, showing some depth.  Nevertheless, the justification is generic and does not compare alternative languages or platforms in detail, so it falls short of a fully developed, âfully justifiedâ rationale.

Python, Flask, requests, OpenAI API and YClients API are combined effectively; modular design and decorators show mastery of language features.  Minor issues in the code snippets (typos, inconsistent variable names) prevent awarding the very top of the range.

The table tests every success criterion, includes concrete test data, expected outcomes and pass/fail conditions.  It distinguishes positive and negative cases (e.g., missing information, unrelated questions).  This level of detail exemplifies a complete test plan.
The report contains no section outlining improvements or future developments informed by testing or client feedback.

A bibliography lists five external documentation sources.  Explanations of key code sections (appointment creation, reschedule workaround, Flask routing) are given, but not every external library or snippet is referenced, and inline citations are sparse.  This meets the âall sources identified, some explanationâ level rather than comprehensive documentation.
No chronological record-of-tasks table, Gantt chart or equivalent timeline is included in any file.  Consequently there is no evidence of how the product evolved over time.

The solution integrates WhatsAppâManyChatâFlaskâOpenAI GPT and YClients CRM, handles multilingual input, rescheduling via recreate-delete workaround, decorator-based dynamic function registration, and error handling.  These features show clear abstraction, third-party API orchestration and non-trivial algorithmic decisions, fitting well into the upper band although not presented with the polish deserving of the absolute maximum 12.

Six measurable criteria cover accuracy of FAQ answers, CRM integration, appointment modification, bilingual responses, robustness to unordered/ missing input, and topic filtering.  They are specific and testable, mapping directly to user needs.  A small weakness is that no performance or reliability thresholds are stated, hence a score of 5 rather than the full 6.
There is no transcript, survey or signed statement showing feedback from the client or adviser.

Code is modular (decorators, separate API calls), suggesting scope for extension, but tight coupling to specific APIs and hard-coded IDs makes modification non-trivial.  Therefore expansion is possible but would require some effort, aligning with the middle band.

The student clearly identifies the real-world client, âHealth 1â dentistry, and briefly quotes both the administrator and CEO, demonstrating that at least two consultations took place.  However, the write-up stops at short quotations; it does not include dates, meeting evidence, or detailed notes of further discussions that shaped the requirements.  This represents clear but limited evidence of consultation rather than the rich, ongoing dialogue expected at the top band.

The written evidence and screenshots imply that key pathways (FAQ answers, appointment booking) work, yet no video or explicit demonstration is provided.  Without direct evidence of full functionality the safest judgment is âfunctions partiallyâ, yielding the top of the middle band.
No reflective section systematically comparing results to success criteria appears in the submission.

annotations.comment: The student outlines consultations with the administrator and CEO but omits dates, meeting minutes or detailed notes. Including specific consultation evidence and dates would strengthen the scenario investigation.
Footnotes refer to appendices but the appendices themselves are missing. Attach actual meeting transcripts or appendices rather than relying on superscripts.
Hard-coded service and doctor IDs reduce extensibility. Parameterize these values or load them from configuration to ease future modifications.
The submission lacks an evaluation section comparing outcomes to the success criteria. Including a structured evaluation would fulfill Criterion E requirements.
No client or adviser feedback is documented. Incorporate signed statements or meeting notes from Health 1 stakeholders to demonstrate real feedback.
There are no future recommendations. Propose realistic next steps (e.g., performance monitoring, new feature integration) to demonstrate reflective planning.
The solution overview rephrases the system purpose but does not link each feature explicitly to client needs. Reference specific client requirements to improve alignment.
The rationale explains why Python, ManyChat, YClients, and ChatGPT were chosen but does not compare alternatives. Discuss at least one alternative platform or language to fully justify choices.
Success criteria cover key functions but lack measurable thresholds (e.g., 90 % answer accuracy or 1 s response time). Defining concrete targets will make evaluation more robust.
No chronological record of tasks (e.g., Gantt chart or development log) is provided. Including a task timeline would clarify how the product evolved over time.
The Table of Contents is empty. Populate it with section titles and correct page numbers for easy navigation.
The test plan table is highly detailed, mapping each success criterion to test data, expected outcomes, and pass/fail conditions. This comprehensive approach is exemplary.
Flowchart 01 provides a clear and comprehensive design overview of user interactions with the WhatsApp chatbot. The annotations guide implementation effectively.
Consider adding a legend to Flowchart 01 to explain symbols and notation. This will improve clarity for readers unfamiliar with standard flowchart conventions.
Flowchart 02 (server-client interaction) effectively captures data flow but could label technology components (e.g., Flask, ManyChat) directly on the diagram.
The hardware relationship flowchart (Flowchart 03) is useful but omits network performance or latency considerations. Adding these would enhance completeness.
Back-end process flowchart (Flowchart 04) is well-structured; consider explicitly depicting data validation and error-handling branches.
Appointment scheduling flowchart (Flowchart 05) is detailed but lacks an explicit error-flow for invalid or missing input; adding this would be beneficial.
The WhatsApp interface screenshot identifies key UI elements but lacks annotations of message formats or error messages. Labeling these would clarify user flows.
The modular appointment-management functions demonstrate good complexity, but asynchronous or batch API calls could further optimize performance under load.
Multiple libraries (requests, parser, datetime) are used effectively, but typos and inconsistencies (e.g., `resp. status_code`) may cause runtime errors. Review variable names.
Bibliography lists key sources but inline citations are sparse. Add references next to code snippets or text to improve traceability of techniques.
Error-handling middleware is described clearly. Consider including a sample log entry or snippet to illustrate how exceptions are caught and recorded.
Key functions lack comprehensive docstrings specifying parameter types and return values. Adding Google-style docstrings would improve code maintainability.
Evidence of functionality is implied through screenshots but no video or execution log is provided. A demonstration video would solidify proof of full functionality.
